---
title: "Dyadic Data Analysis with R"
subtitle: "Part II: Applied Tutorial"
author:
  - "Pascal Küng"
  - "Turu Stadler"
bibliography: ../../vignettes/references.bib
csl: apa.csl
resources:
  - uzh-logo.svg
  - charite-logo.svg
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    css: workshop.css
    include-after-body: branding.html
    footer: "Dyadic Data Analysis"
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    preview-links: auto
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: source
---

## Today's aims and program

| Time | Focus |
|---|---|
| 11:00–12:45 | Introduction and types of dyadic models |
| 12:45–14:00 | *Lunch break* |
| 14:00–15:30 | Estimating cross-sectional dyadic models in R |
| 15:30–15:45 | *Short break* |
| 15:45–16:45 | Intensive longitudinal extensions |
| 16:45–17:00 | *Short break* |
| 17:00–18:00 | Exercises in R |

<!-- TODO @codex: please grey out the first two points, intro and lunch break). -->

<!-- ca. 2 Minutes -->

## Choices of software and framework

## SEM vs. MLM {.smaller}

- Many simple dyadic models can be represented in either framework.
- We use MLM in this workshop, but the best framework is the one that represents
  the complete scientific model most naturally.
  [@ledermannAnalyzingDyadicData2017]

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

### SEM / DSEM is often more natural for

- Latent dyad constructs (e.g., CFM) 
- Item-level measurement models for reflective measures
- Linked outcomes or equations, including mediation and reciprocal effects
- Short dynamic panels requiring explicit treatment of the first observations
- Intensive lagged/dynamic models via SEM/RDSEM

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### MLM is often more natural for

- Parsimonious observed-variable models with modest numbers of dyads
- Nested or crossed grouping and flexible random-effect structures
- Growth or concurrent ILD models with many or unequal numbers of occasions
- Interactions, nonlinear terms (such as RSA)
- Specialized GLMM families, such as zero-inflated, hurdle, and Tweedie models

:::

::::

::: {.subtle}
[@asparouhovDynamicStructuralEquation2018;
@asparouhovComparisonModelsAnalysis2020;
@gistelinckMultilevelAutoregressiveSmall2021;
@delrosarioPracticalGuideSpecifying2025]
:::

## Missing data: not simply FIML vs. complete cases

- SEM commonly offers FIML for partially observed dyads.
- MLM can retain the other observed outcome occasions, but rows with missing
  predictors are usually dropped.
- Multiple imputation is available in either framework. It can be especially
  useful for missing predictors, but is not automatically the best solution
  and must respect persons, dyads, and time.
  [@ledermannAnalyzingDyadicData2017]

<!-- ca. 2.5 Minutes -->

## R vs. MPLUS, SPSS, SAS

Why we teach R: 

- It is open source and free
  - no blackbox, everyone can check the code - and help fix bugs
  - scriptable and reproducible
  - wide variety of free and open packages
  - if something doesn't exist yet, you can create it

Current limitations for dyadic analysis: 

- No SEM package that rivals MPLUS
  - Lavaan/blavaan can estimate many dyadic models but does not support random slopes
  - packages that implement DSEM are available but remain limited in different areas
  
- glmmTMB and brms are some of the most powerful MLM frameworks available
  - Yet they currently lack one desirable feature for ILD models (VAR-style residual structures or kronecker products)
  - SAS and SPSS currently support these and are viable alternatives

<!-- ca. 2.5 Minutes -->

# Estimating cross-sectional dyadic models in R

## Distinguishable APIM



## Exchangeable APIM

## Testing distinguishability

### Wald test for fixed effects equality

### Model comparison

## DIM

## Excursion: non-Gaussian generalized models

# Intensive longitudinal extensions

# 

# Excercises in R


<!--

- DHARMa plots!?! -> as required package for workshop -> link to materials or do in tutorial? Also check AR1 etc., etc. can dharma do this if resid is set to almost zero?

- glmmTMB -> as required 
- dyadMLM -> as required (via pak, see instructions). -> maybe just require pak and we install newest version on workshop-day?
- dplyr -> required
- tibble -> required
- lavaan -> required
- ggplot2 -> required

-------------->

<!-- ILD-part: 

- see dev note on ar1 processes careful to consider degrees of freedom! relevant here?
  - TODO @codex: are degrees of freedom alreaedy important in the cross-sectional case and should we teach anything regarding those?
- see plots on: https://quantdev.ssri.psu.edu/tutorials/apa-ati-intensive-longitudinal-data-session-m-dyadic-multilevel-model

-->

<!-- Excercises part: separate files! Include on overview page?

Create exercises with data visalization, etc. Panel plots, histograms
correlations (wb corr)?

-->
